tag: Forensics

views: 0

excerpt: "A practical DFIR guide to IconCache.db featuring IconCacheReader — real evidence of VMware shared-folder activity, USB drive browsing, and how to avoid overclaiming execution from path text alone."

  - blackbasta-memory-forensics
  - evtx-tampering-recovery
---

## Why This Artifact Still Matters

Prefetch, Amcache, and ShimCache get most of the attention in Windows DFIR, but `IconCache.db` quietly does something none of them do quite as well: it remembers *every path Explorer ever asked an icon for*, including binaries that were deleted, renamed, or only ever lived on a mounted network share, a hypervisor shared folder, or removable media. Because the cache is written at shutdown or reboot rather than in real time, and because Windows almost never proactively cleans it out, entries can sit there for months or years after the underlying file is long gone.

This post walks through what the artifact is, how to pull it apart, and (using real output from the open-source tool **IconCacheReader**) what it actually looks like to find evidence of shared-folder activity in a live case, plus how to avoid the common mistake of overclaiming USB-device evidence from path text alone.

## What Is IconCache.db?

`IconCache.db` is the **path database** behind the icon cache. It lives at:

```
%LOCALAPPDATA%\IconCache.db
  → C:\Users\<username>\AppData\Local\IconCache.db
```

It stores the full file paths Explorer requested icons for, plus a CRC hash that links each path to the actual icon pixel data, which is stored separately in the `iconcache_*.db` image-store files under `...\Explorer\`. That distinction matters for analysts: the `iconcache_*.db` files hold icon *images*, not paths. If you're hunting for file paths, `IconCache.db` is the file you want.

Because it's flushed on shutdown/reboot rather than continuously, it won't give you second-by-second precision. What it will give you is durability: a path can survive well after the binary itself has been deleted.

### How an Entry Gets Written, and Why It Outlives the File

![Diagram showing how IconCache.db gets populated when a file is browsed locally, on USB, or in a shared folder, and why the entry survives file deletion, with three caveat boxes below](data:image/png;base64,)

The mechanism is simple, and that simplicity is exactly what makes it useful: Explorer doesn't care whether the file it's rendering an icon for lives on the local disk, a USB stick, or a shared folder. Any of those trigger the same lookup, and a cache miss writes the same kind of entry. Once written, that entry doesn't get cleaned up just because the file disappears, which is also why the caveats above matter: presence in the cache is not the same as proof of execution.

## How the Cache Has Evolved

| Windows Version | File Name(s) | Typical Location |
|---|---|---|
| 95/98/NT4/2000 | `ShellIconCache` | `C:\Windows\ShellIconCache` |
| XP | `IconCache.db` | `C:\Documents and Settings\<User>\Local Settings\Application Data\` |
| Vista, 7 | `IconCache.db` | `%USERPROFILE%\AppData\Local\IconCache.db` |
| 8, 8.1, 10, 11 | `IconCache.db` (paths) + `iconcache_*.db` (images) | `%USERPROFILE%\AppData\Local\` and `...\Explorer\` |

Modern Windows splits responsibilities: `IconCache.db` keeps the path/CRC index, while a family of `iconcache_16.db`, `iconcache_32.db`, `iconcache_256.db`, `iconcache_exif.db`, `iconcache_idx.db`, `iconcache_sr.db`, and similarly named files hold rendered icon bitmaps at different resolutions. Analysts targeting *paths*, the evidentiary payload, should go straight to `IconCache.db`.

## Parsing It at Scale: IconCacheReader

Manually carving these structures is exactly the kind of thing that should be automated, which is the point of **[IconCacheReader](https://github.com/peli-rufus/IconCacheReader)**, an open-source Python/EXE tool built for incident-response workflows. It runs directly against a mounted forensic image, walks one or many user profiles, and exports table, CSV, or JSON output, optionally enriched with SHA1 hashes and first-run data pulled from `Amcache.hve`.

Key capabilities:

- `-user-profile PATH` / `-users-dir PATH`: target a single profile or sweep an entire `Users` directory
- `-amcache PATH`: cross-reference extracted paths against Amcache for SHA1, first-run timestamp, and product name
- `-csv` / `-json`: structured export for Timeline Explorer, Excel, or a SIEM
- `-include-non-exe`: widen the default `.exe .dll .sys .com .scr .cpl .ocx .drv .mui .ax` filter to catch shortcuts and other file types
- `-recursive`: locate `IconCache.db` in non-standard paths
- Verbose mode reports which internal DB version was parsed (`0x0507`, `0x0506`, or a `fallback-scan` heuristic parser for anything that doesn't match a known layout)

### Running It Against an Acquisition

The screenshot below shows the tool pointed at a mounted acquisition image containing user profiles, parsing `IconCache.db` and pulling out distinct binary paths in one pass: everything from `explorer.exe` and `cmd.exe` to tools staged on a VMware host-shared folder.

![IconCacheReader run against a forensic acquisition image, extracting 364 paths including a cluster of vmware-host shared folder tools](data:image/png;base64,)

That single run instantly maps out the software footprint of the user profile, useful for building an "applications present" inventory before you've even opened the registry.

## Reading the Raw + Resolved Path Pairs

One quirk worth knowing before you load results into **Eric Zimmerman's Timeline Explorer** or Excel: IconCacheReader's CSV schema keeps the *raw* cached string and the *resolved* full path in two separate columns, `raw_path` and `binary_path`. If a viewer only shows one wide "binary_path" column (or the CSV is opened without its header row mapped correctly), the two can visually run together, e.g. `battle.net launcher.exe9C:\Program Files (x86)\Battle.net\Battle.net Launcher.exe`. Loaded correctly, they're just two columns of the same record:

| binary_path (resolved) | raw_path (as cached by Explorer) |
|---|---|
| `C:\Program Files (x86)\Battle.net\Battle.net Launcher.exe` | `battle.net launcher.exe` |
| `C:\Program Files (x86)\Epic Games\Launcher\Portal\Binaries\Win64\EpicGamesLauncher.exe` | `epic games\launcher\portal\binaries\win64\epicgameslauncher.exe` |
| `C:\Program Files (x86)\Microsoft\Edge\Application\msedge.exe` | `microsoft\edge\application\msedge.exe` |

Keeping both columns visible is worth the screen space: `raw_path` is the short/relative form Explorer originally cached, and `binary_path` is the fully-qualified path IconCacheReader resolved it to. Seeing both together helps confirm an entry is legitimate and pin down exactly which installed copy of an application it points to, rather than relying on the resolved path alone.

![Timeline Explorer view of parsed results, showing raw and resolved path pairs for the same cached entry such as Battle.net Launcher](data:image/png;base64,)

## The Big One: Evidence From Shared and Mapped Folders

This is the strongest, most directly-evidenced pattern in this dataset. In a VM-based test environment, the icon cache captured over twenty distinct executables referenced from `\\vmware-host\shared folders\500_installers\`: forensic and IR tooling (`exterro_ftk_imager`, `evtxlogbrowser`, `mftbrowser`, `prefetchbrowser`, `winsearchdbanalyzer`, `vmware-tools`, and more) that were staged on a host-shared drive and simply browsed from inside the guest.

![IconCacheReader terminal output showing 364 extracted paths with a block of cached paths pointing at a VMware host-shared folder](data:image/png;base64,)

What makes this case solid rather than inferred is that the UNC path isn't the only evidence. The same profile's cache directly names the mechanism a second, independent way. A broader extraction against the same profile turned up a shell-namespace entry that Explorer cached verbatim:

```
\Microsoft.Networking.Netbios\VMware Shared Folders::\vmwar...
```

That's Windows' own internal reference to the **"VMware Shared Folders"** virtual namespace, sitting right alongside the `\\vmware-host\shared folders\500_installers\...` paths. It's direct confirmation, not inference from a UNC prefix alone, that the mechanism in play is the hypervisor's host-to-guest file share.

![IconCacheReader run with the -include-non-exe flag showing 533 extracted paths including the shell-namespace entry that names VMware Shared Folders directly](data:image/png;base64,)

That's the forensic value in a nutshell: **`\\vmware-host\shared folders\...`, mapped network drives (`Z:\`, `\\fileserver\share\`), and cloud-sync mount points all leave the same kind of trace in the icon cache.** None of that content lives on the local disk, so it's invisible to file-system timeline tools once the share is disconnected, but the icon cache still remembers it was there. In an incident-response engagement, a cluster of unfamiliar tool names pointing at a shared-folder UNC path is a strong signal that an analyst, an attacker with remote tooling, or a virtualized sandbox interacted with binaries staged outside the protected host. This proves *presence and visibility*, not execution; corroborate with Prefetch or Amcache before concluding a tool was actually run.

## USB Evidence: Drive Letters That Don't Match the System Volume

Jan Collie's 2013 *Digital Investigation* paper, *"The windows IconCache.db: A resource for forensic artifacts from USB connectable devices,"* is the foundational research behind this whole artifact, and it's credited directly in IconCacheReader's Research Credit section. Collie's finding still holds: if a user plugs in a USB drive and simply *browses to* an executable in Explorer, without ever double-clicking to run it, Windows still caches an icon request for it, regardless of whether the file was invoked.

The evidence to look for is a **drive letter that doesn't match the system volume**, cached alongside the normal `C:\...` entries. Here's what that looks like in practice, pulled from the same tool's `binary_path`/`raw_path` CSV columns:

![Results showing D, E, and F drive-letter paths alongside normal C: entries, including an autorun/setup cluster, a staged toolset, and a per-volume Recycle Bin](data:image/png;base64,)

Three details here push this well past a simple keyword hit:

- **`D:\autorun.ico`, `D:\setup.exe`, `D:\setup64.exe`** together are the classic signature of optical or USB installer media: an autorun icon plus 32/64-bit setup binaries at the root of a non-system volume.
- **`E:\vm_cleanup_tools\...`** shows over a dozen distinct utilities (VMware Tools, VMware Workstation, VS Code, WinDirStat, BrowserHistoryExaminer, CleanMgr+) all staged at the root of the `E:` volume, the kind of toolkit that gets carried around on a single external drive.
- **`F:\...\$Recycle.bin\S-1-5-21-...\`** is the strongest of the three: a per-user-SID Recycle Bin only exists on a volume that's been formatted NTFS and mounted with its own file system, not a folder name someone chose. That confirms `F:` was a real, distinct volume, not an artifact of naming.

Each of these still only proves the volume was connected and browsed, not that any specific `.exe` on it was executed. Cross-reference the drive letter against `USBSTOR` and `MountedDevices` in the `SYSTEM` registry hive (plus `setupapi.dev.log` for connection timestamps) to confirm the physical device and connection time, and pair with Prefetch or Amcache before concluding a tool was actually run.

## Beyond Executables: What `-include-non-exe` Adds

By default, IconCacheReader filters to `.exe .dll .sys .com .scr .cpl .ocx .drv .mui .ax`, the extensions most relevant to program-presence and execution analysis. Running the same profile with `-include-non-exe` removes that filter and shows everything Explorer ever cached an icon for. On the profile above, that raised the count from 364 paths to **533**, with the extra 169 almost entirely `.png` files: `StoreAppList.png`, `StoreBadgeLogo.png`, `StoreLargeTile.png`, `StoreMedTile.png`, `StoreSmallTile.png`, `StoreWideTile.png`, `LargeTile.png`, `SmallTile.png`, `Square150x150Logo.png`, `Square44x44Logo.png`: the tile and logo assets Windows caches for **UWP/Microsoft Store app tiles**, distinct from the Win32 executable evidence discussed above.

These aren't incidental noise. A Store-tile asset confirms a UWP app was installed and its tiles were rendered at some point, which is a useful cross-check against `AppxProvisionedPackage`/`InstalledAppXPackages` registry data or `Microsoft-Windows-AppXDeployment-Server` Event Logs when you're trying to establish whether a Store app was present on the system. The default executable-only filter is the right choice for most triage passes, but `-include-non-exe` is worth a second pass whenever Store-app or UWP activity is in scope.

## Adding Amcache Enrichment

Running with `-amcache` cross-references every extracted path against `Amcache.hve`, adding SHA1, first-run timestamp, and product-name columns where a match exists:

```
IconCacheReader.exe -users-dir "E:\Image\C\Users" ^
    -amcache "E:\Image\C\Windows\AppCompat\Programs\Amcache.hve" ^
    -csv results.csv -json results.json
```

A `MATCH_TYPE` of `FULL_PATH` confirms Amcache has a record for that exact path (strong installation/first-run evidence); `NONE` means the icon cache is your *only* trace of that binary, which is often the more interesting row, since it usually means the file no longer exists anywhere else on disk.

## Correlating With Other Artifacts

| Artifact | Primary Evidence | Limitation | Best Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **IconCache.db** | File/program presence, paths | No execution timestamp; written only at shutdown/reboot | Confirming presence, shared-folder & (verified) removable-media activity |
| Prefetch | Execution, run count, last-run time | Missing for many portable apps | Confirming execution |
| Amcache | Installation, file hash, first-run | No direct execution evidence | File identity, install timeline |
| ShimCache | File presence, path | Timestamps don't reflect execution | Confirming existence |
| Jump Lists | Recent file access | Limited retention | Recent user activity |
| Event Logs | Process creation, device connection | May be incomplete | Precise timeline |

The workflow that gets the most out of this artifact: pull the icon cache paths first to build a candidate list of "things that were here," then walk that list through Prefetch/Amcache/ShimCache to determine which of them actually ran.

## Practical Workflow

1. **Acquire**: image the disk with a write blocker; if the machine must stay live, prefer Volume Shadow Copy extraction over restarting `explorer.exe`, which can trigger a cache rebuild and destroy evidence.
2. **Locate**: for each user profile, grab `AppData\Local\IconCache.db`. Use `-recursive` if profiles sit in a non-standard path.
3. **Parse**: run IconCacheReader against a single profile or the whole `Users` directory, with `-amcache` enabled if the hive is available.
4. **Filter and pivot**: load the CSV into Timeline Explorer, filter `binary_path` for `\\vmware-host\`, other UNC prefixes (`\\`), and non-system drive letters (`D:\`, `E:\`, `F:\`) to isolate off-box activity.
5. **Correlate**: cross-reference surviving hits against Prefetch, Amcache, and Event Logs to distinguish "present" from "executed."
6. **Report**: note explicitly that a cached path proves presence/visibility, not execution, and cite which corroborating artifact (if any) confirms the file actually ran.

## Anti-Forensics Notes

Because the file is only rewritten at shutdown or reboot, a sudden absence of expected entries, or a cluster of entries sharing one modification timestamp shortly after a suspected incident, can itself be a signal. It may indicate a deliberate `explorer.exe` restart or manual deletion intended to scrub the cache. Corroborate against Prefetch and Event Logs before drawing that conclusion, since routine reboots produce the same pattern innocently.

## Limitations to State in Every Report

- No precise execution timestamps, only cache-write time.
- Presence in the cache means the file was visible to Explorer, not that it ran.
- The cache can be cleared or rebuilt, intentionally or not.
- Always pair findings with at least one execution-oriented artifact before asserting a program was run.

## Where to Get the Tool

IconCacheReader is MIT-licensed, has no external dependencies for the Python script, and ships a pre-built Windows EXE for analysts who'd rather not carry a Python environment into a case:

**Repo:** [github.com/peli-rufus/IconCacheReader](https://github.com/peli-rufus/IconCacheReader)

```
# Python (any OS)
git clone https://github.com/peli-rufus/IconCacheReader.git
cd IconCacheReader
python IconCacheReader.py -users-dir "E:\Image\C\Users" -csv results.csv
```

The format-parsing logic credits prior research by Jan Collie on USB-connectable device forensics via the icon cache, and Phill Moore & Yogesh Khatri's 2025 write-up examining the `IconCache.db` structure in detail.

## Key Takeaways

- `IconCache.db` records file paths Explorer requested icons for, and that record outlives file deletion.
- **Shared and mapped folders** (VMware host shares, network drives, UNC paths) are the artifact's strongest, most directly-verifiable "external source" evidence, especially when a shell-namespace entry independently names the mechanism, as it did here.
- **USB device evidence** shows up as a drive letter that doesn't match the system volume, cached alongside the normal `C:\...` entries; confirm it against `USBSTOR`/`MountedDevices` for device identity.
- Raw+resolved path pairs in tool output aren't duplication. They're two views of the same cached record and both are worth reading.
- `-include-non-exe` surfaces non-executable cached assets like UWP Store tile `.png` files, useful for confirming Store-app presence separately from Win32 binary evidence.
- Treat it as a *presence* artifact: pair it with Prefetch, Amcache, or ShimCache before claiming execution.
- **IconCacheReader** ([github.com/peli-rufus/IconCacheReader](https://github.com/peli-rufus/IconCacheReader)) automates extraction across single or bulk user profiles, with optional Amcache SHA1 enrichment and CSV/JSON export for Timeline Explorer.
